Overall Market & Sentiment —

Macro: May PCE ran hot on the surface but cooler underneath. Headline PCE +0.4% MoM and +4.1% YoY, first above 4% since Apr 2023 and highest since then; core +0.3% MoM and +3.4% YoY, highest in over two years and well above 2% target. The crucial detail was that the monthly pace matched April and came in slightly below what markets feared. Personal income rose 0.7% and spending rose 0.7%, with the savings rate steady at 3%, a resilient-but-stretched consumer. Oil drove the disinflation narrative: WTI crude fell below $70, down nearly $25 from a month ago and over $40 from its 2026 peak, with Brent back to pre-war levels as tankers crossed the Strait of Hormuz freely. Gas prices dropped to $3.90/gallon. That collapse is the disinflation engine, and it convinced the market the 2026 inflation spike has peaked, sending the 10-year yield below 4.40% for the first time in a month even as the dollar held a 2026 high. The caveat: Trump accused Iran of violating the ceasefire by firing at cargo ships transiting the Strait, a reminder the truce is fragile. U. of Michigan consumer sentiment at 48.9 near historic lows. Next week is holiday-shortened with markets closed July 3; June jobs report Thursday, with forecasts for sharp slowdown from May’s strong 172k.

Micro: Rotation was the story. Russell 2000 gained about 1.0%, Dow rose 0.6% to a record near 52,000, while Nasdaq and S&P declined on tech weakness. Micron surged 15.7% Thursday after strong earnings then fell 6.7% Friday. Apple dropped 6.2% and Microsoft 3.5% Thursday on price-hike news, though Microsoft rebounded 5.71% Friday with Salesforce +5.45% and IBM +5.08%. Eli Lilly jumped 7% on EU approval for its leukemia therapy; Moderna rose nearly 12% on investor day; Visa and Walmart each gained over 2%. Losers included Caterpillar -5.67%, Cisco -4.56%, Goldman Sachs -4.07%, and ON Semiconductor -22.58% on its Synaptics deal. Alphabet joined the Dow; SpaceX fast-tracked into Russell 1000. Q1 earnings season wrapped with around 28% growth.

Sentiment: VIX near 18.9, still complacent. Gold rose 1.1% to roughly $4,092 as lower yields boosted non-yielding assets. Crypto Fear and Greed Index collapsed to 13, deep in extreme fear. Quarter-end rebalancing added cross-currents after the spring rally. This week featured rotation and broadening in stocks, bond rally on disinflation hopes, and crypto at fresh cycle lows amid AI fatigue and OpenAI IPO delay weighing on megacaps.

The market changed character this week. For four months AI megacaps carried the tape; fear was they would crack and drag everything down. This week they finally cracked, on valuation fatigue, on Apple and Microsoft raising prices, on OpenAI delaying its IPO. And yet the market did not break. Money rotated, hard, into value, small caps, industrials, financials, and health care. Dow hit record while Nasdaq fell; large-cap value beat growth by nearly 4 percentage points; breadth improved with 63% of S&P above its 50-day average versus 50% at start of June. Macro quietly improved underneath: oil collapsed below $70, gas fell to $3.90, the 10-year dropped under 4.40%, and hot-but-cooling PCE showed the inflation peak is behind us. This disinflation gives Fed room. But crypto made fresh cycle lows with Bitcoin weakest since Oct 2024 and Ethereum breaking its 2026 floor, ignored by rotation and hit by strong dollar. Consumer sentiment near record lows at 48.9 with 3% savings rate now facing price hikes proving costs are being passed on. Cheaper oil helps but soft sentiment plus sticky core inflation remains the quiet risk. June payrolls Thursday before holiday; forecasts show hiring slowed sharply from May’s 172k. If jobs cool moderately and oil keeps falling, this rotation foundations a broader healthier rally with AI names consolidating. If jobs collapse, disinflation turns to growth scare and cyclicals reverse. Broad rally or unwind depends on labor market in coming weeks.

LIVE NEWS REPORT — SUNDAY, JUNE 28, 2026

U.S.–IRAN STRIKES PUT HORMUZ DEAL IN DANGER

The U.S. and Iran traded new attacks around the Persian Gulf after a commercial tanker was hit near the Strait of Hormuz.

U.S. officials said American airstrikes hit Iranian missile, drone and coastal radar sites. Iran answered with missile and drone attacks aimed at U.S. military facilities in Bahrain and Kuwait.

No U.S. casualties were reported in the first accounts. Bahrain reported damage in civilian areas. A Qatari citizen was reported killed by shrapnel in a related incident.

President Donald Trump warned Iran against further escalation. Vice President JD Vance said the U.S. would “win either way.” Iran’s Revolutionary Guard threatened more retaliation if Iran is hit again.

The biggest unknown is whether the interim U.S.–Iran agreement can survive. Talks are now strained by military action, shipping security, sanctions, and Iran’s nuclear program.

GULF STATES CONDEMN IRANIAN ATTACKS

Kuwait, Bahrain, Jordan, the UAE and Italy condemned Iran-linked attacks in the Gulf region.

Bahrain hosts the U.S. Navy’s Fifth Fleet. Kuwait called the attack a violation of sovereignty.

The Strait of Hormuz remains the central pressure point. About one-fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas shipments move through that route.

The next issue is whether outside navies expand patrols, whether Iran continues restricting shipping, and whether regional mediators can keep talks alive.

ISRAEL–HEZBOLLAH CEASEFIRE UNDER PRESSURE

Israel carried out new strikes in Lebanon against Hezbollah targets despite a recent U.S.-brokered ceasefire effort.

The renewed fighting complicates U.S.–Iran diplomacy because Iran has linked regional security, Hezbollah, and Israel’s posture in Lebanon to any wider settlement.

Hezbollah rejected parts of the Lebanon-Israel arrangement, calling it a surrender of sovereignty.

What remains unclear is whether the latest strikes become a limited flare-up or pull Lebanon, Israel, Iran and the U.S. into a wider chain reaction.

TRUMP PICKS LANCE SCHROYER TO LEAD ICE

President Trump said he will nominate Lance Schroyer, a former Oklahoma state trooper and U.S. Marine, to lead Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

Schroyer has about 29 years of law-enforcement experience. DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin praised the choice and urged Senate confirmation.

ICE has not had a Senate-confirmed director since 2017. David Venturella is currently serving as acting director.

The nomination comes during a major immigration crackdown and after months of controversy over detention deaths, enforcement tactics, and civil-rights concerns.

COURT KEEPS STRICTER SOOT POLLUTION RULE IN PLACE

A federal appeals court rejected the Trump EPA’s effort to abandon a Biden-era rule tightening the national soot pollution limit.

The rule lowered the annual fine-particle pollution standard from 12 to 9 micrograms per cubic meter.

Fine-particle soot is linked to asthma, heart disease, hospital visits, and premature deaths.

Supporters of the rule say it could prevent thousands of early deaths and hundreds of thousands of asthma symptoms each year. Industry groups and Republican-led states said it could raise energy and manufacturing costs.

The EPA said it is reviewing the decision.

KENTUCKY FLOODS KILL AT LEAST FOUR

Flash flooding in Kentucky killed at least four people after heavy rain hit parts of the state.

Gov. Andy Beshear said three deaths were in Madison County and one was in Jackson County.

Some areas received up to 7 inches of rain. Parts of southwestern Indiana saw 4 to 10 inches.

Emergency crews carried out water rescues from homes and vehicles. Officials warned people not to drive through flooded roads, especially at night.

A landslide near a dam embankment in Bullitt County led to evacuations, though officials said there was no sign of dam failure at the time of reporting.

IDAHO HIT BY HAIL, FLOODING AND MUDSLIDES

Severe storms in Idaho flooded streets with rain and hail, leaving some suburban roads looking like ice-covered rivers.

The National Weather Service reported several inches of small hail and more than an inch of rain in parts of the Boise area.

Owyhee County recorded 553 lightning strikes. Ada County had one of its highest June lightning counts since 2000.

Winds above 50 mph caused damage across the Treasure Valley. Canyon County declared a disaster emergency. In Payette County, flooding triggered mudslides and blocked roads.

HEAT RISK BUILDS TOWARD JULY 4

NOAA’s Climate Prediction Center warned that extreme heat is likely to continue into the July 4 holiday period across large parts of the central and eastern U.S.

Forecast heat index values could exceed 100 to 105 degrees in many areas.

Warm nights may limit overnight relief, which increases risk for older adults, outdoor workers, children, people without reliable cooling, and people with heart or lung disease.

The National Weather Service office in Paducah, Kentucky, warned of an extended hot and humid period, with lows in the mid-70s giving limited overnight relief.

EBOLA RESPONSE STILL STRUGGLING IN CONGO AND UGANDA

The Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda remains a major health emergency.

The outbreak involves the Bundibugyo strain, which has no approved vaccine or specific treatment.

Reuters reported more than 1,100 cases in Congo and 20 in Uganda. Africa CDC raised the estimated response need to $1.4 billion.

Health workers face violence, mistrust, displacement, and difficult access in conflict-affected areas. Nearly 300 Ebola-positive people were reported unaccounted for in Congo.

WHO says testing has expanded sharply, though the outbreak is still moving faster than parts of the response.

AI COMPUTING DEAL SHOWS RACE FOR CAPACITY

Australia’s Firmus Technologies signed a strategic partnership with Nvidia to help provide cheaper access to AI computing power for emerging AI companies.

The deal reflects a bigger global pressure point: advanced AI needs massive computing capacity, chips, data centers, and electricity.

A separate Goldman Sachs estimate cited by Axios said global AI infrastructure investment could reach about $7.6 trillion from 2026 to 2031.

The next AI bottleneck may not be apps. It may be power, chips, cooling, data centers, and who gets affordable access.

WORLD CUP KNOCKOUT ROUND BEGINS

The 2026 World Cup moved from group play into the round of 32.

Canada and South Africa opened knockout play today. The U.S., Canada and Mexico all advanced from the group stage.

The U.S. is scheduled to face Bosnia-Herzegovina on July 1 in Santa Clara, California.

The tournament’s expanded 48-team format means more underdog stories, more third-place calculations, and a longer knockout path to the July 19 final in East Rutherford, New Jersey.

ALBANIA PROTESTERS FIGHT KUSHNER-LINKED COASTAL PROJECT

In Albania, activists are trying to block a coastal development project linked to Jared Kushner.

The protest movement has drawn attention for using flamingos as a symbol, because the project could affect nearby habitat.

The fight combines environmental protection, tourism development, local anger, and global political attention because of the Trump family connection.

The key question is whether Albania’s government allows the resort plan to move forward or whether public pressure slows it down.

Government cut its own tax on petrol and diesel by ₹10 per litre, giving up ₹1.55 trillion in revenue. Refiners pushed cooking gas production up 50% in one week to replace lost imports. 

A gas cylinder costing ₹1600 to import was sold to families at ₹942. Restaurants and factories got rationed so households came first. Not petrol pump ran dry - Government paid so 330m homes did not have to

India buys far more from the world than it sells. That gap, called trade deficit, runs at 8% of GDP - Take oil out, it falls to 5%, Take oil and gold out, 3.5%. 

Foreign investment hit record $95bn in FY26 but most left again, net money that stayed was only $7.7bn. India UK trade deal going live July 15 will drop taxes on 99% of goods. Good start but India needs to make more things at home.

Goldman Sachs just upgraded India 2026 growth forecast to 6.8%. When oil shipping through the Persian Gulf got blocked, global oil price spiked from $69 to $126. 

Now with peace talks it has dropped to $78, India economy still grew 7.8% in first quarter. Total money leaving the country versus coming in, called current account deficit, stayed low at 0.6% of GDP. 

India got through the worst oil shock in 50 years. Next step is building piped gas networks and converting coal into gas so India needs fewer imports.

$GLD Goldman Sachs: “Gold Is Not Done”—Why Wall Street Still Sees Upside

Gold has pulled back sharply from its record highs, but Goldman Sachs believes the bull market is far from over. Despite a nearly 30% decline from January’s peak, the bank continues to forecast $4,900 per ounce by year-end, implying roughly 22% upside from current levels.

The message from Goldman is clear: the recent correction is viewed as a pause, not the end of the long-term bull market.

Why Has Gold Fallen?

Gold’s weakness has been driven primarily by higher inflation and rising interest-rate expectations.

As oil prices increased and inflation concerns returned, investors began pricing in the possibility that central banks may keep interest rates higher for longer.

Because gold pays no interest or dividend, higher bond yields make fixed-income investments relatively more attractive, putting short-term pressure on bullion prices.

This explains why gold, silver, platinum, copper, and palladium have all struggled over recent months.

Why Goldman Remains Bullish

Goldman argues that several powerful long-term drivers remain intact.

The biggest is central bank demand.

According to the World Gold Council, 45% of central banks surveyed plan to increase their gold reserves over the next 12 months, the highest level on record.

Central banks continue buying gold because it provides:

• A hedge against geopolitical uncertainty.

• Diversification away from the U.S. dollar.

• Protection during periods of financial instability.

Unlike retail investors, central banks typically buy for strategic reasons and often hold their positions for many years, creating a stable source of demand.

Structural vs. Cyclical Drivers

Goldman believes gold benefits from both structural and cyclical forces.

Structural drivers include continued central bank accumulation, rising government debt, and long-term demand for safe-haven assets.

Cyclical drivers could emerge if economic growth slows, inflation eases, or central banks eventually begin cutting interest rates. Lower interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding gold, historically supporting higher prices.

What Investors Should Watch

The next major catalysts for gold include:

• Federal Reserve interest-rate decisions.

• Inflation data.

• Central bank gold purchases.

• U.S. dollar strength.

• Geopolitical developments.

If interest rates remain elevated for longer than expected, gold could continue facing short-term pressure. However, if monetary policy becomes more accommodative, the precious metal could regain momentum.

Bottom Line

Goldman’s outlook suggests the recent selloff reflects changing interest-rate expectations rather than a collapse in the long-term investment thesis.

While short-term volatility is likely to continue, the bank believes structural demand—particularly from central banks—remains strong enough to support higher prices over time.

For investors, gold continues to serve a unique role within a diversified portfolio. It may not generate earnings or cash flow, but during periods of inflation, geopolitical uncertainty, and financial market stress, it has historically acted as an important store of value.

Whether gold reaches $4,900 will depend largely on the path of inflation and interest rates, but Goldman Sachs believes the precious metal’s long-term bull market is not over yet.

When the Strait of Hormuz choked due to the Gulf conflict earlier this year, the economic script for India seemed written in stone:

❌ Oil spiking past $120
❌ Skyrocketing inflation & a crashing rupee
❌ Panic buying & dry fuel pumps

After all, India imports 90% of its crude oil and over half its LPG. But instead of disaster, something unusual happened: Nothing. No lines at petrol pumps. No empty kitchens. No 1991 or 2013-style macroeconomic panic. Why? Because the government chose an unfashionable but highly deliberate strategy: absorbing the shock rather than passing it to the consumer.

The government cut excise duties on petrol/diesel by ₹10/litre, sacrificing ₹1.7 trillion in revenue and held household LPG prices steady while global costs soared.

Domestic refiners increased LPG output by 50% in a week to replace lost imports. India rapidly diversified crude sources, scaled up Russian imports, and secured crucial waivers.

Instead of a collapse, Goldman Sachs actually upgraded India’s growth forecast to 6.8% for CY26. Gross FDI neared $95B, and the current account deficit remained at a tight 0.6% of GDP.

Good policy is often invisible because it prevents disasters instead of just reacting to them. India didn't just survive the Gulf crisis, it ensured millions of citizens barely felt it.

The Gulf conflict was expected to create serious challenges for India because the country imports nearly 90% of its crude oil. Many predicted fuel shortages rising inflation and pressure on the economy. Instead India showed the strength of years of planning.

• Fuel stations continued operating without disruption.

• LPG supplies remained available across the country.

• Refiners increased LPG production by nearly 50%.

• Crude imports were diversified to reduce dependence on one route.

• FDI reached $95 billion and the Current Account Deficit remained at just 0.6% of GDP.

• Goldman Sachs raised India's FY26 growth forecast to 6.8%.
India proved that resilience is not built during a crisis. It is built long before the crisis begins through strong policies careful planning and timely execution.

The final two days of the quarter are here with stocks up early as tech shares rebound slightly from their pullback. Though June has been a tough month, the S&P 500 Index is just 3% off all-time highs, and there've been glimmers of strength in non-tech areas.

"Some of the selloff in the tech space may be related to quarter-end rebalancing by major market players such as pensions and sovereign wealth funds," said Nathan Peterson, director of derivatives research and strategy at the Schwab Center for Financial Research (SCFR), in his Weekly Trader's Outlook. The focus this week could turn slightly away from tech and toward the U.S. economy ahead of Thursday's June nonfarm payrolls report. That's a day earlier than usual ahead of Friday's U.S. closure for Independence Day.

Major indexes fell Friday and the tech-dominated Nasdaq Composite's losing streak reached five sessions. Choppiness returned as the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) flirted with 20. Any move above that early this week might signal more weakness for the market, with investors focused again on Treasury yields and oil as the Middle East remains unsettled. The status of the ceasefire is a fresh wildcard after several weekend skirmishes, though the U.S. announced Sunday that hostilities are paused. This appeared to underpin early Wall Street gains.

Trump Announces Iran Talks, Iranian Official Denies They Are Scheduled After Weekend Strikes.

President Donald J. Trump announced upcoming peace talks with Iran, but Iran’s Deputy Foreign Minister has denied any such meeting amid escalating tensions and violence.

PULSE POINTS

❓ WHAT HAPPENED: President Donald J. Trump claimed on Truth Social that Iran has requested peace talks, set to occur in Doha, Qatar, on June 30. However, while some Iranian sources have told Reuters that technical talks are set for Tuesday, Iran’s Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi has publicly denied the claim, stating, “No technical meetings of the working groups are planned for this week.”

📺 DETAIL: White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said Special Envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner would lead the U.S. delegation if talks proceed. The conflicting statements came after both countries exchanged military strikes over the weekend, escalating tensions and raising concerns about the future of nuclear negotiations. According to the White House, the latest violence followed Iranian attacks on commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz using drones, prompting U.S. strikes on Iranian missile, drone, and radar sites along the Persian Gulf. Leavitt said the United States would respond forcefully to attacks on commercial vessels and reiterated the administration’s commitment to protecting international shipping. Oil prices rose amid the unrest, with Brent crude trading around $72 per barrel and West Texas Intermediate near $70. U.S. negotiators are reportedly seeking an agreement that would require Iran to abandon its nuclear weapons ambitions and surrender its stockpile of enriched uranium, while Iran has called for joint control of the Strait of Hormuz and the release of billions of dollars in frozen assets. Iranian state-linked media and outlets affiliated with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) have argued that the country should pursue a nuclear deterrent.

💬 KEY QUOTE: “Violence will be met with violence… there were attacks on commercial vessels that the United States of America, directed by the President, responded to,” said White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt.

From ChatGPT: No. The claim that the United States is down to "only 4 weeks of oil" is misleading unless someone is referring to a very specific stockpile and ignoring normal production and imports.

There are three different things people often confuse:

The Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) — the government's emergency crude oil https://t.co/eKarbWnAvb of late June 2026, the SPR held roughly 330 million barrels, the lowest level since the early 1980s after recent emergency releases.
The U.S. consumes about 20 million barrels of petroleum per day, so 330 million barrels equals about 16–17 days of total U.S. consumption if it were the only source.

Commercial inventories — oil held by companies.U.S. commercial crude inventories were around 412 million barrels in late June 2026.
Adding commercial stocks to the SPR gives more than 700 million barrels of crude in storage, though not all of it is immediately usable in an emergency.

Actual U.S. energy supply.The United States is one of the world's largest oil producers, pumping roughly 13.8 million barrels per day, while also importing and exporting millions of barrels daily.
The country does not depend solely on stored reserves for its energy needs.

So, if someone says "America only has four weeks of oil left," that is generally false as a description of the nation's total energy position.
If they mean government emergency reserves plus certain assumptions about imports stopping and domestic production being disrupted, they may arrive at a figure of several weeks—but that is a specific worst-case scenario, not the normal reality. The U.S. continues to produce large quantities of oil every day and remains a major participant in global energy markets.

Mid-Day Markets - June 29, 2026

This Morning's Top Story

The macro landscape is locking down into a high-stakes waiting game today, June 29, 2026, as institutional desks brace for tomorrow's massive, dual-pronged USDA data drop. The entire financial complex is at a near-standstill ahead of the June 30 Acreage and Quarterly Grain Stocks reports. 

This landmark release is expected to completely reset the baseline supply math for the remainder of the calendar year, forcing automated trend-following models and commodity trading advisors (CTAs) to run highly defensive, lightened books into the end of the quarter.

Exacerbating this end-of-month caution is a rapid cooling of consumer demand metrics across the physical livestock sector. Last week's post-peace-accord equity melt-up has officially met structural retail resistance. Friday afternoon's cold storage data revealed that domestic beef stocks failed to register their typical late-spring drawdown. 

This clear signal of slowing consumer clearance paired with a sharp breakdown in premium wholesale cutout values—has heavily dampened recent supply-side panic, sending meat futures into a corrective retreat as the market tests reality past the upcoming July 4th grilling peak.

Market News (Performance Review & Rebalancing)

Early-week capital allocation is dominated by book-squaring and risk-off adjustments ahead of the quarter-end government tape.

Grains Frozen Pre-Report: The row-crop complex is trading with an incredibly static, defensive tone this morning. July Corn is bound to a tight 2-cent range, drifting quietly at $4.13 per bushel. Traders are completely unwilling to stake fresh capital as analysts predict the USDA will expand soybean area at the explicit expense of corn relative to March intentions. July Soybeans are holding a highly fragile floor near $11.04, balancing strong new-crop export commitments against near-perfect midwestern vegetative emergence.

Livestock Stalls on Cutout Collapse: The meat pits are enduring a sharp technical pullback today, erasing a chunk of last week's explosive placement-driven gains. August Live Cattle are down heavily, trading near $245.80, while August Feeder Cattle have surrendered ground to hover at $369.85 per hundredweight ($369.85/cwt). 

The selling pressure is a direct response to a massive Friday afternoon collapse in wholesale choice boxed beef values, which plummeted a steep $5.29 to print at $391.01/cwt, proving that retail buyers are severely bucking against all-time high beef costs.

Crude Drifts Under $71: WTI crude futures are extending their structural, post-de-escalation slide today, opening the week soft to trade near $70.04 per barrel. 

The smooth, unhindered flow of commercial maritime traffic through reopened Middle East shipping channels has entirely cleared out any residual transit risk premiums, steadily dragging down baseline energy margins.

Key Levels (The Map)

Grains

Corn (/ZC): Support 400 - Resistance 420 - Status: Flat Consolidation; July futures pinned at $4.13 as the trading floor awaits tomorrow's 12:00 PM Eastern structural acreage reset.

Soybeans (/ZS): Support 1090 - Resistance 1125 - Status: Pressured; July contracts drifting at $11.04, heavily capped by month-end position-squaring and crashing crude oil inputs.

Wheat (/ZW): Support 565 - Resistance 595 - Status: Bearish; July Chicago wheat soft at $5.76 as rapid winter harvest completion across the Southern Plains brings steady physical hedge volume to the market.

Livestock
Live Cattle (/LE): Support 242.00 - Resistance 248.00 - Status: Corrective Retreat; August contracts falling to $245.82 as sluggish country cash negotiations fail to establish early-week momentum.

Lean Hogs (/HE): Support 93.00 - Resistance 97.50 - Status: Mixed Short-Covering; August contracts holding at 96.57 cents, catching a steady bid after Thursday's USDA report revealed a smaller-than-expected total U.S. hog herd.

Energy & Metals
Crude Oil (/CL): Support 68.50 - Resistance 73.00 - Status: Heavy Trend; WTI crude sliding down past the $70.04 mark as global shipping lanes run completely normalized.

Gold (/GC): Support 4320 - Resistance 4390 - Status: Quiet; Flat lining into quarter-end as macro fund managers lock in baseline corporate bond allocations.

Chart of the Day: August Feeder Cattle (/FCQ26) Technical Setup
Action: Defensive Hedging Re-Lock / Defend Baseline Cash Flow.

Why: August Feeders have pulled back sharply below the $370 threshold today, experiencing clear profit-taking. While the long-term 10% placement drop remains a massive supply-side reality, the near-term board is reacting to a cooling retail market. When choice boxes drop over five bucks in a single afternoon, packers will instantly reduce cash bids to defend their own operational margins.

Tactical Note: The contract is undergoing a standard technical retest of its 100-day moving average. Feedyard managers should treat this corrective flush as a strategic warning shot to lock in trailing floor protection before retail demand metrics cool further post-July 4th.

Market Pulse & Strategy
The smart money is moving with absolute seasonal discipline this afternoon. Institutional managers are executing aggressive profit-taking sweeps across livestock length, recognizing that the combination of sticky cold storage inventories and collapsing choice boxes signals an immediate, near-term demand ceiling. In the agricultural room, a total structural gridlock has taken hold. Commercial end-users and international grain buying houses have pulled their bids completely, content to stand entirely aside until tomorrow's noon government report establishes the true physical balance sheet for the 2026/27 marketing year.

Pro Takeaway: The demand side of the ledger is reasserting its authority over supply metrics. While the cattle countryside is structurally thin, yesterday's carcass values prove that packers will aggressively throttle processing speeds the second wholesale margins compress. Keep your trailing defensive hedges locked down tightly, and do not make the mistake of front-running tomorrow's high-stakes acreage numbers.

The paper market is putting on a masterclass in risk management today. Seeing August Feeders get smacked back below $370 proves exactly what we suspected—the board simply cannot outrun a five-dollar collapse in boxed beef values. Packers are looking at a backup in cold storage and fading retail margins, and they are going to use that exact excuse to slow down cash cattle trade this week. Over in the grain room, July Corn is just staring at the wall at $4.13. It is a completely dead, un-tradeable tape until the USDA drops the hammer tomorrow at noon. Keep your powder dry.

Notes

The Cold Storage Reality Check: The quiet macro story of the weekend is the USDA’s latest cold storage data, which showed that total U.S. beef inventories for May failed to track their historical late-spring drawdown. This confirms that despite lower slaughter counts, high retail prices are finally forcing the domestic consumer to ration steak and premium cuts. For Western Corn Belt operations holding heavy, long-fed inventories, this demand drag means packer patience is going to intensify as we enter the mid-summer slowdown.

The Rest of the Week

Tuesday, June 30 (12:00 PM Eastern): The blockbuster USDA Acreage and Quarterly Grain Stocks reports. This will trigger massive, automated volume spikes across all grain sessions.

Wednesday, July 1: Weekly EIA crude oil and ethanol inventory datasets, tracking domestic blending metrics against a sub-$71 crude baseline.

Trader Strategy: Maintain maximum defensive stop parameters on all remaining long grain layers, leaving your baseline accounts completely clear ahead of tomorrow's acreage volatility. In livestock, pull your trailing stop-losses up aggressively to insulate the core profits gained from last week's rally, keeping your expectations firmly aligned with a slower, packer-controlled country cash trade.

The Strait of Hormuz Oil Crisis: What the Market Isn’t Pricing In As of June 29, 2026

The Buffer Is Gone — Or Nearly So
The IEA’s emergency release of 400M+ barrels began depleting in March and is exhausted by July 9. By mid-July, the full extent of all temporary buffers — Russian floating stocks, Iranian floating stocks, IEA reserves — will be gone. The remaining market adjustment needed is 7.1 million barrels per day, roughly 16% of global crude trade.
JPMorgan warned that OECD inventories could reach “operational stress levels” in June and hit “operational minimum” floors by September — that’s the bare minimum oil needed for pipelines, storage tanks, and terminals to even function.

The Math Is Brutal
The entire 400M barrel IEA emergency release equals just 20 days of normal Hormuz flows, or four days of global consumption. It was always a psychological Band-Aid, not a structural fix.

Replenishment Is a Fantasy Right Now
Global inventory drawdowns since the start of the conflict have already consumed approximately 400 million barrels of existing stockpiles, with further draws expected through summer 2026 even as Hormuz shipping slowly resumes. Replenishing those stockpiles requires roughly an extra 1 mb/d of supply for the next three years on top of underlying demand growth. Three years.

The Farce
Oil is sitting at ~$73 — essentially flat to pre-war. The market is pricing in a clean resolution that does not exist. Ships are getting hit by drones. The IMO evacuation convoy was paused after a cargo vessel was struck by a projectile on June 26. Iran controls the approved routes and has warned that vessels using any other corridor are “in range of missiles.” And OPEC countries’ production has fallen more than 30% since the start of the war.
The most dangerous word in markets right now is “gradual.” Every official projection assumes gradual reopening, gradual recovery. But there is nothing gradual about a ship getting struck while using the IMO-sanctioned safe corridor.

What Happens in One Month
Mid-July is the hard deadline when every temporary buffer expires simultaneously. If the strait is not functionally open — and right now it is not, with only 31–70 verified commercial crossings per day versus 120+ pre-war — the market faces a 7.1 mb/d structural shortfall with nothing left to cushion it. That triggers an oil price spike, inflation re-acceleration, and Fed paralysis in the same news cycle. That is the definition of stagflation — and it is currently unpriced.
The SPY is not reflecting this. The bond market is not reflecting this. And the clock runs out in approximately two weeks.

The market is pricing weak Chinese demand today. If China unexpectedly shifts from drawing inventories to importing aggressively again while the world's spare capacity is shrinking and OPEC cohesion is weakening, oil can reprice very quickly. https://t.co/0H0WgnmXJQ

Oil and gas markets are caught in a tug-of-war between a fragile U.S.-Iran truce and fresh military clashes, with prices steadying as shipments resume despite ongoing attacks.

Oil Prices Steady After Weekend Strikes

Brent crude is trading around $72.50/bbl, while WTI hovers near $70.00/bbl. Prices rose slightly on Monday after renewed U.S.-Iran strikes, though Brent is still down about 10.6% from the previous week.

Strait of Hormuz: The Flashpoint

The Strait remains the central geopolitical risk. Over the weekend, the U.S. struck Iranian targets in response to drone attacks on commercial vessels. 

Despite the attacks, Middle East producers are continuing oil and LNG loadings. Traffic surged last week to its highest level since the war began, but remains well below pre-conflict levels. 
U.S. and Iranian officials are set to resume talks in Qatar on June 30.

Iran Accelerates Exports

With a 60-day U.S. sanctions waiver in place, Tehran is rapidly ramping up exports, loading simultaneously at both terminals at Kharg Island. 

Over the weekend, about 8 million barrels of Emirati and Qatari crude moved out on four Very Large Crude Carriers (VLCCs).

Iraq Pushes for Higher OPEC Quota

Iraq is aggressively pushing OPEC for a higher production quota, driven by an economic crisis from the war and billions in new investments from majors like BP, TotalEnergies, and ExxonMobil. 

Iraq's output plunged to just 1.48 million bpd in May and has threatened to leave the bloc if its quota isn't significantly increased.

Russia's Fuel Supply Concerns

President Putin convened a meeting with officials and oil majors to discuss domestic fuel supplies. Gasoline stocks are at 1.7 million metric tons, and a potential full ban on diesel exports is under consideration.

Global Response & Market Impact

Shipping costs have spiked, with war risk premiums rising nearly 50%. The IEA has coordinated a 400-million-barrel strategic reserve, while Asian economies are diversifying suppliers and Europe is turning to coal. Analysts note an "uneven reopening" with a downward bias on prices if it continues.

People keep saying "data is the new oil."

They're wrong. It's worse than that.

Oil gets extracted once and it's gone.

Data compounds.

Every Nigerian transaction, conversation, health record, and behaviour pattern fed into a foreign AI model makes that model smarter, permanently.

The model improves. The value accumulates. Elsewhere.

Nigeria doesn't get a royalty on the intelligence extracted from its people's data.

There is no OPEC for data.

There is no sovereignty framework.

There is no audit of what foreign AI companies take when they train on African content.

This is the resource extraction story of the 21st century.

And it is happening right now, without most people noticing.
